ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general hazelnut entry (FOODON_03301710); no blanched-specific sub-entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers hazelnuts generically; no blanched-specific facet code available. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 256319004 covers hazelnut as a substance; blanching preparation not distinguished at this level. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of blanched hazelnuts is approximately 30 g (about 20 whole nuts), providing roughly 189 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Blanched Hazelnut — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F09030022 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 12120, Hazelnuts or filberts, blanched) was cross-referenced to validate macro and micronutrient plausibility; values are consistent.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value specific to blanched hazelnuts is published in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 15 is estimated from the published GI of raw/whole hazelnuts (GI ≈ 15, low category) as documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database entry for hazelnuts. Blanching removes the skin but does not alter the macronutrient composition materially; the GI estimate is therefore considered defensible. GL per 30 g serving = (15 × 17.0 × 0.30) / 100 = 0.77, rounded to 0.9 accounting for available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: Zero sodium and high potassium (658 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Blanched hazelnuts are suitable for individuals with hypertension; their zero sodium content and high potassium (658 mg/100 g) support a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles, but portion size should be limited to ~30 g/day due to high caloric density (629 kcal/100 g).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~15), high dietary fibre (11 g/100 g), and dominant MUFA content slow postprandial glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Blanched hazelnuts have a very low glycemic index (~15) and a negligible glycemic load per 30 g serving (~0.9), making them appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (~30 g/day); the high fibre and MUFA content attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High MUFA (oleic acid ~45.65 g/100 g) and low saturated fat (~4.5 g/100 g) with zero cholesterol and zero sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Blanched hazelnuts are cardioprotective when consumed in moderate portions (~30 g/day); their dominant monounsaturated fat profile (oleic acid), zero cholesterol, zero sodium, and high dietary fibre (11 g/100 g) are consistent with LDL-lowering and cardiovascular risk-reduction evidence from nut consumption trials.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High MUFA/PUFA ratio with documented anti-inflammatory properties; vitamin E and proanthocyanidins (in skin) support inflammation reduction
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Blanched hazelnuts qualify for the MUFA/PUFA clinical carve-out as a whole nut with saturated fat ~4.5 g/100 g and dominant oleic acid (~45.65 g/100 g); they are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, though raw hazelnuts with skin provide greater proanthocyanidin antioxidant benefit than blanched forms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blanched hazelnuts are whole hazelnuts (Corylus avellana) from which the outer brown papery skin has been removed by brief immersion in boiling water followed by rapid cooling and mechanical rubbing, yielding a smooth, ivory-white kernel. The texture is firm and crunchy with a sweet, buttery, mildly nutty flavour that is slightly milder than skin-on hazelnuts because the tannin-rich skin has been removed. Nutritionally, blanched hazelnuts are energy-dense (629 kcal/100 g) and dominated by monounsaturated fat (approximately 45.65 g/100 g, primarily oleic acid), which is associated with LDL cholesterol reduction and cardiovascular protection. Total fat is 61.15 g/100 g with saturated fat only ~4.5 g/100 g. Protein content is 13.7 g/100 g and dietary fibre is high at 11 g/100 g. Carbohydrate is 17 g/100 g with only 3.49 g/100 g sugar, giving a very low glycemic index (~15) and negligible glycemic load per serving. Sodium is zero and potassium is 658 mg/100 g, making them suitable under DASH dietary guidelines for hypertension management. Calcium (149 mg/100 g) and iron (3.3 mg/100 g) are notable micronutrients. Blanched hazelnuts are widely used in confectionery, baking, praline, gianduja chocolate, and as a snack. They are vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. The primary allergen is tree nut. Clinically, they are appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease management in controlled portions (~30 g/day). Blanching removes the skin which contains the highest concentration of proanthocyanidins; raw hazelnuts with skin provide greater antioxidant benefit.
common_user_queries: Are blanched hazelnuts good for diabetics?
Difference between blanched and raw hazelnuts
Are hazelnuts good for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F09030022, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Values cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central NDB 12120 (Hazelnuts, blanched) with consistent results. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, clearly documented. Saturated fat, MUFA, and PUFA values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cross-reference and documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (45.65 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (7.92 g/100 g) are not reported in the HPB source record; these values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 12120 (Hazelnuts or filberts, blanched) and are consistent with published hazelnut fatty acid profiles. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 2. Micronutrients vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these fields are not reported in the HPB source record for this entry; detailed vitamin and mineral panel data for blanched hazelnuts are available in USDA FoodData Central but were not included in the HPB canonical record. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for hazelnuts (raw/whole); blanching does not materially alter macronutrient composition and the estimate is considered defensible. GL per 30 g serving calculated as (15 × 17.0 × 0.30) / 100 ≈ 0.77, reported as 0.9 using available carbohydrate. 4. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' in the input cultural.dietary_tags has been noted but is not applicable to hazelnuts; this tag appears to be a data entry error in the source record and has not been carried forward. 5. No zero-macro justification required: protein_g (13.7), fat_g (61.15), and carbohydrate_g (17.0) are all non-zero. Sodium_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; hazelnuts are naturally sodium-free. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no dietary cholesterol.
